Okay, it is 12.01am Eastern Standard Time. I am Christine Potter and with me is Mark McRae, Kevin Halbert and our special guest, Tyler Bramlett. I am going to hand things over to Mark to take it away. Thanks Christine. And today guys, we are very fortunate to have Tyler with us. And I want to tell you a little bit of a story of how we got Tyler. When Kevin Bond and myself were putting together the program, we wanted to try and find not only the best people in every niche or what they are known for, we wanted to find what is really happening right now. Because there is no point in bringing you things that worked even a year ago or two or three years ago and Tyler is one of these people. And how I came to meet Tyler is quite interesting. I knew that I wanted someone who was on a cutting edge of email marketing right now. And I spoke to a few people and a few people were more than happy to join us but it just didn't feel like it was really happening right now. And eventually I got a hold of one of the account managers in Clickbank and asked them to go through all of their clients, all of their vendors and try and find me the very best person in email marketing right now. And we came up with Tyler. So Tyler and I got started chatting and today this is one of the things I am looking forward to more. You know, everybody has the thing that they would like to look forward to. I am really looking forward to what Tyler has to share with us because I have an inclination of what it is. And if you listen carefully to what he has to say, I think you are going to find this very valuable. So welcome Tyler. Well, thank you so much for the great introduction. I really appreciate you guys inviting me to be a part of this. Now Tyler, a lot of people may not be aware of you right now. So how did you get from where you were? I mean, what did you do before and how did you get involved with online marketing? Sure. Yeah, I have been in the fitness industry for about six years now. I started some boot camps in my local area and really kicked it off hard, did really well with the boot camp stuff. But there was a pivotal moment in my boot camps that actually ended up playing a really big role in my internet career later on which was when I got a pretty bad injury. And I used to be very involved with the boot camps going there, selling people face to face, really being involved with the business and I had got a really bad injury that left me on the couch. And it was basically like doctors orders and I'm allowed to move for two months. So it was like learn to run a business from your laptop when you used to do face to face stuff or, you know, lose all your money and have to get kicked out of your house and follow you. This is one of those, you know, gun to head moment stories that I think people really do quite well because innovation is there when you really need it, you know. And so I learned how to take everything I was doing face to face like a sales process and turn it into an automated process. So that people went through the same emotional steps I was trying to get them through in a face to face process and it worked out really well. And before you knew it, we were making more money than ever before and I was working less and less and less and less. And then the guy I partnered with, I started noticing his true colors when he saw me working less and less. He's kind of an old school businessman like FaceTime, be there as many hours as you possibly can. And I'm kind of like a new school guy where it's like automate, automate, automate, automate, and so we didn't see eye to eye after a while. So I ended up selling the business to him and in the meantime, before I actually sold the business, I had started an online company with the intention of kind of building it in order to do the same sort of stuff on an online facet rather than just through a face to face within our local community. So I started the online business and that was only about three years ago, a little bit less than three years ago, and have just been building it and building it and building it really fast and paying very close attention to the details, particularly in the relationship with my email list. And the way I used to describe it is I'm always looking to develop a one to one relationship. How do I communicate with people on my email list in a way that they feel good about it and then still sell them every day and so on and so forth. So that's what I'm hoping to teach you guys today. Well, that's what I think is the amazing part is that I mean, nearly everybody who will hear this has some kind of email marketing and auto responder. But you've taken it to a new level. I mean, the people I've spoken to have told me that, you know, if there's an ounce of blood left in that email, you've got it. I mean, you've basically figured out how to extract the maximum amount of value from any group of emails. So how did you, you know, you never just came to that. There must have been a learning process or how did that happen? Well, so one of the challenging things I noticed, Mark, is that there's a lot of people who are really great at copywriting. And then there's a lot of people who are really great at data. So in one world, these are the people writing the sales letters, creating the sales funnels, writing emails. In this other world, these are the people buying the media, analyzing the data, deciding to what to do with the funnels based on the data. And there's not a lot of people that can straddle both worlds. And I just happened to be kind of a natural born salesman and really good at math. So it was one of those things where I was able to test very efficiently and adjust the copy by myself without having to have two different parts of my business. So it allowed me to kind of streamline things where, you know, say I had so many thousand people on my email list, I started testing lots of different stuff, different types of subject lines, different types of way of treating the people who open, who click. And I basically failed my way forward. You know what I mean? I've done a lot of mistakes, a lot of mistakes, a lot of mistakes. Because there's been times where I test this and they I'm like, this is going to kill. And then we end up bouncing like 10% of all the emails we sent because it just got it up by spam filters like crazy. So I also know what not to do as well as what you, you know, at least from some perspective. So it really just came from lots of testing, Mark, trying different things, how to elicit reactions out of people in certain ways. And a lot of times our emails flopped, but now we have a pretty solid arsenal of what works really well. And the cool part is you can apply it to pretty much any industry. It doesn't matter if you're selling fitness stuff, money based stuff, pretty much anything. I've even helped several people with multiple seven and even eight figure businesses just build better rapport with their list by choosing to email market to them in such a way that speaks to what their previous history of actions was. So that's, that's what I did was just, just test stuff out. And actually, you know, the real moment that kind of happened that kind of forced me, another gun to head moment was when, when I had a list of about 4,000 people was when I sold my bootcamp business. And so I sold the bootcamp business and I'm looking at this list of about 4,000 people. And I'm saying, gosh, I got to make $20,000 a month off this. Otherwise I'm going to be slipping backwards from where I was at before I sold this business. And so it was just like, how do I, how do I do that? How do I make $20,000 a month off a list of 4,000 people? And it was another gun to head moment of just like, let's figure this out. And we were mainly promoting third party offers and stuff like that. And just tested stuff until it worked and mailed pretty hard. But in a way, like I said, that makes people feel good, doesn't make people feel bad about you. Well, that's what I'm excited about to hear. But where does it start Tyler? Does it start with the squeeze page? Is there a squeeze page? Do you do something different there? Or. Okay. So yes, for that, you know, I'm going to walk it through like the basics. So we will start at the strategy point. And like, that's what I really like to start with, with anybody I'm doing consulting work with or anything like that. Now it's just basically strategy. Because I don't remember who said it, but I remember they said strategy trumps copy. Right. And crapy copy is amazing. Copy is amazing. I love it. It absolutely kills. But if you have amazing strategy, you can beat someone with amazing copy. No reason I say those because I have friends who are much better copywriters than I am that run email lists kind of similar to the way I do. But we make a lot more money per customer than they do because we have a better strategy behind it. Now, if you marry that to great copywriting, now you have just an insanely winning combo that can just absolutely decimate. So it starts with strategy. So I ask people, how do you get your emails? Are you collecting customers? Are you collecting leads? Because this is really important. You're going to treat your leads significantly different than you're going to treat your customers. Our data shows us that leads are about one fifth to one eighth as valuable as customers per month on a revenue basis. So we'll make about 40 cents per lead per month. We'll make about $3 per customer per month. So a lot of times our strategy with the leads that we're generating is, hey, we can mail these leads all we want and try to get 30, 40 cents per month off of them, or we can try to convert them into customers and we can multiply that value significantly. So that's where the first step is trying to take people from leads to customers, especially if your goal is to make consistent revenue through email marketing, because they're just so much more valuable. They really, really are. They've opened their wallets, they've raised their hands and they voted yes for you. And that means they're going to trust you. They're going to put their trust in you significantly more. So really just understanding that first piece of the puzzle. Are they coming from a squeeze page? Are they coming from a product purchase? And then the second piece is, are they coming from a media buy? Are they coming from search traffic? Or are they coming from affiliates? Because those three different avenues are going to react significantly different to an email us as well. And this is just kind of building a big picture because everybody's going to have some combination of squeeze page, customers, SEO, media buys, affiliates, and learning how your particular list reacts is the most important part. Right. And that ultimately comes down to a simple question. All right. Why are you emailing them? So first you have to have your strategy in place. And to me, the reason why I'm so important is because I have a lot of friends. Maybe they're making multiple six figures, multiple seven figures, but they'll, you know, oh, I'm going to try YouTube out. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do, oh, I'm going to do Facebook ads. And then we're going to do a podcast and like, and they forget what their business model is, their strategy is. And the most successful friends I have are so simple. They're like, we buy media to this product. We collect customers. We mail third party offers. Rinse, repeat, rinse, repeat, rinse, repeat, rinse, repeat. Right. And they just keep doing that over and over again. So it doesn't really matter if you're collecting leads and converting them to customers. If you're collecting customers directly, if you're getting them from media buys, SEO affiliates, the point is pick an avenue and get really damn good at it. Right. If you're, if you're getting affiliate traffic, then go to the bars with people, buy drinks all night long, get more affiliate traffic, be the coolest guy in the world and just make good friends and just get more affiliate traffic. If you're SEO, learn how to do SEO or learn how to create great content that people's content worth sharing. If you're doing media buys, get really good at media buys or hire a really great media buyer to drive traffic down your chain. Just pick an avenue and stick with it. That way you can develop your strategy on the back end, which is where your big money is going to be made. Okay. So why are you emailing them? That's really important. Are you going to sell them products? Are you going to sell them continuity? Are you going to build a relationship and set a stage for future selling? Or are you going to sell third party offers? That's what I kind of break it down into. And a lot of times people don't actually know why they're emailing their lists. I'm sure you guys have met people like this, right? Where they send an email and you're like, what was the purpose of it? Email. And they're like, well, I don't know. I just had to send an email. No, you don't have to send an email. You should never send an email without a reason for sending the email, right? It should be something. It should be to sell your product, to sell some sort of continuity program, to build a relationship and pre-frame the next product you're going to sell, or to sell third party products. I'm not a fan of just sending content unless that content has a reason because it's just a waste of an email. You could eventually, you could have sold them on something. And always having a call to action, I think is really important within every single email you ever send. I think people are afraid of, you know, mailing too often. I think people are afraid of damaging their list. The reality is there's always going to be someone on your list who tells you to like shut up and die. You know what I mean? I'm sure you guys have had that before. I have that all the time. And it's just a part of the internet itself. People hide behind their screens. And so what they ultimately want to do is they want to come out and lash at you because you're doing better than they are or whatever. So get all the naysayers stuff out of your head. Quit looking at that negative stuff. Have your customer service team get rid of all the negative stuff and have them forward you all the positive stuff. That's what I do. So every day when I open the customer service, I'm like, oh, yeah, we're doing such good things. I tell them this before me, some of the really ridiculous ones where they're just saying the most heinous stuff out there because it makes me laugh too. Anyways, so really like where are you getting the emails? Why are you emailing them? If you can't answer those two questions, then you got to start back at square one. Not ultimately a square one. You know, where are you getting the emails? Why are you emailing them? So any thoughts or questions about that foundation before I jump onto the next section here? The only thing I would say is, can you guys hear me? Yeah. Yeah. The only thing I would say is that me and Bon had a very different philosophy. A lot of people say you got to know your list at least once every 21 days. That was actually back in the old offline days, but me and Bon had a very different philosophy and we said we aren't going to speak up unless we have something to say. And so we speak up so infrequently that we're the opposite of everyone's philosophy that you have to always be present in their mind. And so we've always gotten, when we talk to the email service providers, they're always like, you're open right through the roof. Right. And it's because we don't talk unless there's something to say. Yeah. And we don't just say something just to remind them we're here. And I think that they just see that as a rare moment. Sure. You know what I mean? Well, I think that also comes down to, so this is a really good point because we're talking about strategy again, right? What's your strategy? Your strategy is to speak up only when it's time to speak up. And when you do, shout it from the damn rooftops, right? And then you absolutely dominate it when you do that. I'm sure of it. I have a different business strategy. We're in the fitness industry. So people are constantly looking for the next new thing, constantly for the next new thing. So our strategy is to mail internal and third party offers. And the best way to make revenue on that that we found is to mail every single day. And I have people in my industry who are happy about, you know, hey, we have a 36% or a 40% open rate and we mail once a week. And I say, okay, well, that's great. You know, we have a 19% open rate and we mail 10 times a week. Right. And I type, I tag out all my people, guess what? A lot more than 36% of my people are opening the list and several of them are opening and clicking three, four or five times in that week. So the ROI is significantly higher from those people than if it is just mailing less, but it comes back down to strategy. What's your strategy? Know it, know your list, know your strategy, know your purpose and just do it. Just keep running with that. Don't guess and switch things over like that. So I think that's a really good point that there's different strategies and both work really well. Let me ask you another question. Do you, when you, there's some people, um, we've been in back room meetings with some of the big boys and sometimes they get into these funnels and this would be four funnels, we'll call it funnels. These are the guys that basically invented these funnels. And it was like four years before they became all the trend and it was like, this was just, you know, secret stuff. But I remember they, they were like, you know, it's, you know, they, they got them all the way through the funnel, but anything that didn't get through the funnel that was at the top, they would just, it was like a buyer, diabetes, shit out of the list kind of attitude, you know, right? Because those people, it was just like a tomb. And did you have any hail marries? Just, just throw out there to go, let's see if these people are even alive. You know, with those names that have never done anything. Do you ever do anything crazy to just see anything will happen? Do you segment the list of dead, dead non-responders? Yeah, absolutely. So when you have like the dead non-responders, we do, I'll show you this kind of later with the users and stuff. We'll do, we'll do kind of private style emails, like really one to one. Hey, Kevin, it's Tyler. Just wanted to see if you wanted to grab this, one of these freebies below, pick A, B or C, just hit reply and we'll send it back to you. So it's really more of like an engagement builder and that respect when we have people who haven't opened in a while, people who've been on our leads list for six months, haven't bought anything. And, you know, it's surprising that I'll give you a really simple example. One of the best leads list emails we ever sent was a personalized email that said, Hey, Kevin, you know, we send you all this great content, all these great podcasts and interviews and things like that. And you never bought anything from us. And it takes money to do this stuff, man. Like I put a sale on this product for nine bucks. You think you could buy it just to support us? Boom, one of the best emails we ever wrote to our leads list right there. We just basically say, can you give us some money already? And people respond because they're like, Hey, you know what? He's right. I've been taking all their free content for a long time and I'm not giving him anything in return and he's asking for nine dollars. Right. And what really we're just trying to engage them, move them over to the customer side, raise their hand. So what was the subject line to get them to open? Were these opens, but no buys or were these not even non-opens? These are, these are non buyers. These are all like leads list people. They're opens, but no buyers. Yeah. So it didn't have to be a special subject line to get them open. They would open. They just ignore it. No. And the most of the time we'll use, we'll use like, we use a, you know, a merge field, like a contact first name in a privatized subject line. And I've tried a billion different things. The most effective one I've ever found is, is by first name frowny face. That's, that's an email open rate monster. And I've had other people test that to probably over a million emails and everybody's like, Whoa, this email is crazy. But B Y or B Y? B Y E. And then Kevin or, you know, first name or whatever it is, and then frowny face. And the best part is, you know, you have to change your subject lines and Google if you're resending to unopens, because otherwise it'll clump your data into one feed. So you can just change a smiley face. So I actually have a file that I found online where it shows you like hundreds of different texts based smiley faces. We just change the smiley faces around and it works really good. So anyways, there's another little fun, little quick secret. That we use that does really well. We'll use it next week. Yeah. Try the buy the buy first name. Anybody out there, if you guys have any sort of like time capped, quantity cap promo, the last email, you know, buy, buy Kevin frowny face. And it says, Hey, Kevin, I want to let you know you have to say goodbye right now to this product, because it's going to double in price or the doors are going to close or you're losing out on copies. So, you know, click here to buy the damn thing. They goodbye. Yeah. So that works really well. So, um, well, let's jump in the second, second section there so that we don't spend all day, you know, we can spend all day at the end here, but, um, building a relationship is, uh, is really important. And a quote that comes to mind for this kind of section is most new entrepreneurs fall in love with their products, most successful fall in love with their customers. I really love that. That's a really powerful thing because everybody I talked to is just in love with what they created. And that's okay. I get it. You know, we all create things for a living. And when we do, we're probably really proud of it. Sometimes it flops. Sometimes it does well, right? But the reality is it's not the products that make you successful. It's the people that believe in you that make you successful. And so instilling that belief back into them that they can succeed will cause them to reciprocate. So really, you got to build a relationship with your people. If you don't have a relationship where they feel like they can email you and ask you a question and then get a response from you, then it's probably not a list that that's going to provide very much value to your bank account. So I'm a huge fan of really building a solid relationship. And so some of the stuff we do to build an autopilot relationship through our kind of customer intake process. I like to get to know who they are and what they want. And a simple example of this is here is one of our pages. This is actually the one we're running for this launch right now. So they grab this ads product, right? And there's a video here. I'll kind of explain the video script in a second, but we frame this as the client intake form. Now, mind you, we already have their data. We already know their email and we know their name and stuff like that that we get from their purchase data. But this client intake form gives us first name, last name, cell phone, gender, age, biggest obstacle, biggest goals, health histories. So we know who has diabetes, Alzheimer's, heart disease, joint pain, joint injuries, hair loss, vision. And then they basically segment themselves into a bunch of different buckets, which is just absolutely wonderful when it comes to our being able to mail third party offers. A simple example is we mailed up third party offer, which was a diabetes offer to a list of 1600 people that we knew had diabetes. And we ended up making $2,100 on the first drop to that list, which to me is wonderful because people say, oh, you sent 1600 clicks and you made $2,100. And I said it since then 1600 emails and made 20. Oh, so 1600 people open. No, no, 1600 emails were sent and we made $2,100. So it's so powerful when you can basically say, Hey, yeah, I know you've got diabetes, right? Here's a horror story about when I was a kid and I saw somebody who had diabetes, get their leg cut off and all this stuff. I don't want this to happen to you. Here's a great product in that you should check out, you know, that kind of thing. Right. So if you can communicate on this one to one, you already know who they are, what they're interested in, what their health concerns are. It's super powerful. Now the drawback was is we couldn't just say, um, Hey, you've got to fill out this segmentation form so that we know what to mail you third party offers after you join our customer list. Right. That's just lame. Right. So I wrapped my brain for a while about how we can make them feel good about segmenting themselves. So a, we call the client intake form because that's what we do in personal training worlds. We have a client intake form where we sit and ask questions. And then the video script is very particular. The video script, it goes a little something like this. It's like, Hey there, thank you so much for purchasing product XYZ. And, uh, you know, most people out there just ask for your name and email address, which tells me that they don't care about you. Right. And we do things a little differently around here. We actually care about who you are and what your goals are. So I created a client intake form below that you can fill out to let us know who you are, what you're interested in, so that way I can provide valuable content based on you as an individual. So just fill it out, take you 30 seconds, click submit, and I'll see you at the demo page. Oh, now we've pre-framed this thing. We changed it around completely in their minds. They're like, he cares about me and he wants to know more about me. So this is just a magical thing that, that we've kind of used for the last few launches. And now we have just this amazing ability with our list to mail offers that appeal to men. And when people email, they say, Hey, can I buy an email drop off to you? I said, what's your target demographic? They say men in their 40s, 50s and 60s who want to build muscle. I say, okay, great. I got 6,500 of those people, you know, and we negotiate a deal from there. So it's really, really cool to be able to segment your list in so many different ways. And then if you have customers on your list that are already on your list and you don't have this data, then what you just got to do is create some sort of free offer and you pump them through the segmentation form to get a free offer in order to get to know more about your people. So then you can start segmenting more and more and more. And the more people you have segmented, just the more it just becomes so frightening, easy to make more money. And you can tell people exactly who they are in the email or presuppose an email exactly who they are so that they don't know they're the only one getting the diabetes email or the men email, the women email, so and so forth. So that's the first step when we take on any customers, we dump them into that segmentation form and we, we, we ask them to tell us who they really are. Um, the second step is kind of in your auto responder sequence. And I'm just going to talk about an auto responder from just the indoctrination relationship building, cause there's a whole pile of things you can do behind it. But this is kind of the key part right here, because to me, this is your competitive advantage, right? And so after you segment them into a form, you need to share your story and tell them why you're different, right? So whatever industry you're in, you need to make them think you care about them. Right. And the reality is most people online marketing aren't, aren't devious people. We're actually trying to help people, right? Whatever industry we're in, health, fitness, money, things like that. We're trying to teach people what we know, what we actually do for a living. So it's, it's amazing if you can actually convey that across through stories in a way that makes people feel rapport with you. And I use Dan Kennedy's really simple guide, guideline for rapport. If people are emailing you back with the same story you're sharing them, but it's their version of the story, then you're really hitting some chords, right? And so that's one of our guidelines. Hey, if we can get one out of a hundred people to email us back and say, you know, thanks so much. This story resonated with me. I just, same thing happened to me. Then absolutely, you've got your finger on that person. They trust you. You're their guru, so on and so forth. So sharing the stories and why you're different are super huge. And a simple example of somebody I'm working with as far as their immediate autoresponder, they had this, you know, generic autoresponder. Hey, thanks so much for grabbing our product. And then the next day it was like, Hey, you should grab this product. And the next day it was like, you should grab that product. And hey, you should buy this product too. And this supplement and everything. It was like, come on guys. And their average stick rate on their list was like 80 days, right? Between customer and opt out. So all I did was share a story for three days, which was like, Hey, thanks so much. You know, we're a family owned company. Here's a picture of my family. That's my son, my daughter. That's that's my grandson and everything. And really build that rapport with them. This has like been 30 days and their list is already up to 105 days retention. So we gained like 15 or sorry, 25 days of people on that list, of people staying on that list. And they're a very high volume front end company. So this is going to dramatically accelerate the amount of money they're going to make in the long run, especially as we see the time go on because the data is so new. It's just affecting the last few people to go through it. So, um, so, yeah, so build a relationship by sharing your story. And everybody's heard this before, but I'm going to say it again. Facts tell stories. So right, just share your story. Make it emotional as possible. Drive the knife deep. And if you have multiple stories that can, that can resonate with multiple people who are all your target demographics, then share one in each email and toggle it a little bit. So you can share a story about this pain point that resonates with this person and this pain point that resonates with this person. You want to get to really build that rapport by just sharing stories and really, um, talking with them in a way that, that sets you apart from everybody else. Right. You're not just the other fat loss guy. You're this guy. And the reason why you care more than everybody else is this, right. Um, and then the next step, you and our autoresponderers, we always send them a personalized email on the autoresponder, uh, that, that even says, you know, like, Hey, I know this looks like a canned email, but it's not. This is really from us and it is a candy email, but, um, we send them that because I ultimately my goal for inbox deliverability is to create a dialogue in Google. And this is a challenging thing to do because your customer service team has to be reliable enough to respond to the emails that we're getting. So a simple thing might be on day three. Hey, Mark, uh, thanks so much for, for joining the customer list. I like to email all my new customers personally and just ask them what's your number one goal is here, apply and let me know. Yes, I know this looks like a candy email, but it's not, you know, hit me back and I promise I'll, I'll answer this personally. And then, uh, we have our customer service team really educated in, in ways to help them respond and educate and sell. So, Hey, here's how you solve this problem. By the way, have you checked out our product X, Y, Z? It really can help you solve this problem even faster. And that's one of the best ways we can create a dialogue in their account, get them to open our emails, get things like Gmail to like us delivering to their inboxes because they see we're a real person communicating with them in their inbox list. Um, the other thing we do with a, a media autoresponder is to give them a reason to open all of your emails, right? And I racked my brain about this a while back too, where it was like, why would I open like every single email somebody sent me? So I said, okay, people buy our stuff because we like, they like workouts. So when we just created a meme and we put it at the bottom of every single email, it's an infographic that's the workout of the day. And that way in the autoresponder, we say, Hey, guess what? We're going to give you a free workout every single day, 365 every single day of the year constantly. And all I want you to do is, you know, try out the workouts that you want to do and then create a folder in your email inbox and go ahead and file all my emails away in there. So at the end of the year, you'll have a treasure trope of awesome workouts you can follow along to. So again, this comes down to, we all know that if you can get people to create folders, respond to you, add your name to their contacts list, you're going to be significantly greater chances of getting in their inbox, their priority inbox, I should say, rather than their spam filters and so on and so forth, especially when you get into high volume sending. And by changing the way that these things are framed, people feel good about it. They add you because you're their new friend. They put your things in their folders because you're giving them something every single day of value that they can file away. They respond to you because you ask them, Hey, what's your number one goal? And then after that, you responded with a legitimate answer. And if my customer service team can't answer them, I answer them personally. And that's not as often nowadays because we've built such a large file of calling responses, but I try to make sure that everybody's taken care of. So that's the initial, the initial auto responder. And then the last piece is pre framing what's to come, which comes back to your strategy. So in Kevin's case, that's like, Hey, we only email you when we want you to know something. Absolutely. In my case, I'm like, Hey, I'm like the wandering workout warrior and I'm always trying out new things. I'm going to email you every single damn day and you should buy a lot of this shit because that's how I learn by buying a lot of shit myself. Right. So it's a pre frame that complements the strategy. That makes sense. So any, any thoughts on that or ideas about just that building relationship thing? No, it was out of a J Abraham thing, wasn't it? Well, if the customer. What's that? You know, that was just at Benson's house the other week. And I think Jay said that and I was writing that down. Yeah. Oh, that's great. I don't know if you're going to cover a later Tyler that indoctrination sequence. How long is it? How long do you normally? So short. That's the problem. People overestimate how long these things needs to be typically five to seven days. Every day, five to seven days. Every day, five to seven days. But again, comes down to your strategy. Don't email every day. If your intention isn't to email every day, email the amount of times that you intend to send consistently. The only reason I tell people in my industry to email seven days a week is again, it's a perceptive issue. A, I can pretty much guarantee we make more money on third party offers. If you email every single day versus emailing like two or three days a week, and I have a lot of data to show that that's probably accurate, right? In the health industry, I should say specifically. The problem is, is let's say you get on my list and I email you three days a week. Right. And then I start emailing you seven days a week. How does that make you feel? I go, well, you flood my inbox. What the hell are you doing this for? Get the heck out of my inbox. Let's say I start mailing you seven days a week and I start mailing you three. How does that make you feel? Well, where'd he go? Why isn't he sending me an email every day? Right. So it just, it just comes down to the perception again. I never want the perception to be negative. I always want to be positive. So bare minimum start at seven. If you feel like that's not your business model, you can always go down without almost any pushback vice versa. It's kind of hard to go from three to seven and not get a lot of pushback initially. So not like it can't be remitted. I've not thought about it. I think about it. Agora has really got that, haven't they? Yeah. Yeah, they're fantastic. They know exactly what they're doing and their strategy and that's maybe why they're so powerful. Oh, you know, most of these companies, I was just talking to the lead copywriter from Motley Fool and he's talking about all the different strategies they have in place and it's so specific. It's so like, this is exactly what we're going for. And so many people, I think they, like I said, they just forget about that section one, what's your strategy? Why the heck are you emailing these people? And if you don't know like chess game, you know, five steps ahead, what should the reason why you sent email one is because email five has got this down the line, then, you know, you're not, you're not maximizing the ROI you can get from your email list. Sure. Yeah. Good stuff. Right on. Well, let's, let's talk about behind this, this auto responder sequence. So this, this email marketing template here can be applied to auto responders. If that's your goal, I prefer to mail my people on broadcasts day to day. And the reason why is because I love to be intimately tied to the data because the data to me speaks to what my people are interested in. Are they opening? Are they clicking? Are they, are they reading? Are they buying? Right. So I really like to be intimately tied to the data. And this again comes to the strategy where we don't have a continuity program right now we're working on implementing that one in the next two months on the back end of our, our initial auto responder sequence. Once that happens, then the entire conversation about that was going to change. And we're going to hit the continuity for 60 days, probably before dropping anybody onto a third party or internal product email list just because the value is so much higher there. So again, that comes down to the strategy of you guys, but I'm going to talk to you guys a little bit about what I call the seven levels of email marketing. And this is all about the relationship you're building with your people, through the way you communicate with them. And the goal isn't to go from like level one to level seven. The goal is just to kind of go to level one to level two, two to three, three to four, four to five. And if you can hang out between five and seven most of the time, you're going to see a significant bump in the amount of revenue you'll be able to build on your email list. Right. And it's sad because a lot of people really do hang out like one, two or three. Right. So I'm just going to read through these. This is the time I'm teaching on my email marketing mastermind later this year. So I'm going to give you guys a little taste of what I've been working on here. So level one is you have no relationship and all you think about is how much money you can make off your list. Right. So this is, this is no way to treat people who put food on your table. Again, this is just married to the cash, not married to your customers. Right. Level two is you have a little bit of personality, but your writing sucks. Right. This keeps your email sounding impersonal and makes people not really interesting, interesting hearing from. Right. So this is kind of your average email marketer. They're not really creating a really good reputation or rapport with you and stuff like that. Email number, or level number three is you've built rapport and you've built influence over them. So this is kind of like a huge step. And to be totally honest, this is where most people hang out as, as this level number three. They have a little bit of personality in their emails. They're a pretty good writer and okay writer. They have some rapport with their people to the point where their people will listen to them, but maybe they don't have all of the game that I'm going to share with you right here. So level four is you've built rapport and influence and you have a system of engagement so that they feel a direct connection to you. That's huge. Right. That comes down to the personalized emails that I'm talking about, sending these things sparsely, not continuously, but sparsely and making them really powerful when you do send them. Sending them at the right times and the right times could be anything from, holy crap, I hammered my list super hard with that offer. I need to send a personalized email that gives them something free and apologizes for hitting them so hard to the last email in a launch campaign where you're like, buy Kevin, you know, you got to buy this stuff before it's gone. Right. So it just really comes down to when to use that stuff. But the point is you've built rapport influence and you have a system of engagement and engagement simply means that they engage in the dialogue with you, they engage in the call and response with you. Right. Level five, you've built rapport and influence. You have a system of engagement and you've segmented them in relationship to who they are. So they only see what's relevant to them. That's even more fun. So now the simplest step in the fitness industry is all you do is segment men versus women. You're going to be able to do a lot more with your email list than the guy who doesn't know who's men, who's women. And that's because no guy wants to buy the nine day skinny gene program. Right. And no girl wants to buy the pack on 30 pounds of muscle in 30 days program. I shouldn't say no girl or guy. I'm sure this guy's want to fit into some ladies want to get buff. So point is you can nail people based on what they're actually interested in. Level six, here's where it gets kind of fun. Right. So you've built rapport and influence. You have a system of engagement. You've segmented them to the point where you know who they are and you segment promos on a weekly basis to provide relative content based on what the user is determined. And ultimately at this point they feel like you're their close friend emailing them and that you're so in tune with them mentally and emotionally. And I'm going to explain what I call user-determined email marketing. Some people call it automated segmentation, other things like that. But I do this on a broadcast basis and I'll show you why this is so powerful from a psychological angle in a second. And the final step is you've built rapport and influence. You have a system of engagement. You've segmented to the point where you know who they are and you segment promos on a daily basis to provide them relevant content based on what the user is determined. So in tune with them mentally and emotionally because you understand exactly what they're thinking and what they desire. Right. And this comes down to really understanding the path that they just went through. Right. Because what happens with most people regardless of the industry is they develop a series of email marketing and sequential campaigns. Right. I'm going to have email one, go to video one, email one, two, go to video two, email three, go to video three. And then we have three final emails. Well, what happens if somebody didn't watch video one? What happens if somebody didn't read the initial launch email, but they read the middle one? You know, it's just you're coming into the game, not prepared for the next step. So writing sequential campaigns is great if you can get each person to take each individual step in sequence. But if you can't get them to take the steps in sequence, then it becomes a mute point because people are opening things that are not relevant to what they've just seen. Right. So that's, that's where that level seven piece comes into play. And I'll explain the user to determine email marketing thing at the very end. And then we can just kind of open a dialogue and how this can apply to anybody's business, regardless of their strategy. But the first thing I wanted to do was talk a little bit about a concept I use called phishing. And this is particularly for people who have a larger email list and who mail more frequently and who want to maximize their ROI because I get this question a lot, which is like, Hey, Tyler, how do you make, you know, $3 per customer per month and whatever, like a consistent basis. And I'm like, well, I don't mail offers that don't work very well. You know what I mean? That's the reality of it. Like, so if I could sit there and commit on my calendar, you know, hey, I'll commit three days to you and then I'll commit three days to an offer that does like 20% of what another offer could do. Well, then I'm just losing money. And to be totally honest, when I was making $200 a day, I didn't really mind the day when I would make a 15, 20 bucks. If I was a loss, whatever, but it's not that bad. You only make $5,000 a day and you make $300 the next day, you're pissed off. You're like, he just stole $4,700 for me right now. So, so yeah, so this concept of phishing is different. What we do is we segment our list into different buckets. We have a few different buckets for our customer list. We have our leads list and we have different partnership lists that we have with other vendors where we basically, they provide email data and we do our email marketing campaigns behind it. Right. And so what we do is we have a daily revenue goal for each one of these lists. And what we can do now is we can mail five different offers at the same time on a Monday, right? And I could test five different offers, some sort of curiosity, basically get the click email. And I can know which one's going to perform the best based on the data that we see that day. The next day I could take all five lists and go and turn it towards the one offer that does significantly better. And the reason why I call this phishing is like, imagine you go out with your body is phishing and there's like six ponds around you, right? And you all toss your fishing rods in and you're fishing and you want to reel it up and he's got a gold fish and next guy reels it up and he's got a turtle. The next guy reels it up. He's got like a little piece of moss on there. He's like, what's this? And then the fourth guy reeling it up and he's like, holy cow. And he's got a 60 pound bass on the other thing. He's like, what are you going to do? You're going to sit there and you're going to fish in your gold fish pond? You're going to toss your rod in the 60 pound bass pool, right? So that's why I call it the concept of phishing because it's really, you're just fishing for offers that your people resonate with. And once you find the offers that your people resonate with, good God, you can do so much better than you would ever imagine. And like a simple example from my own business in the last month is, you know, we've mailed offers that have done like 400, maybe 450 bucks, right? To like a drop and we're like, oh God, I should have made thousands, right? And then we've mailed offers that did so well on the first email, like over five figures in one email that we just resend unopens the different subject lines. We have one email that's at $36,000 this last month from one email. Right? Why would I want to stop sending that email when resend number six did better than drop number one to this guy's offer, right? So anyways, the concept of phishing is really important, especially when you're when you're when you're mailing lots of different things, when your goal is to mail every single day, when you're mailing third party offers, internal offers, you're looking for the email creatives and the offers that resonate the most with your list so that you get the maximum ROI out of ROI to people. And when you find that 60 pound bass, you just turn all the traffic at it and you go and you go and you go and you go until it burns. And then you go to the next one, right? You start phishing again. So that's a really important step in really kind of getting the maximum revenue out of your list. And the last piece I kind of want to share with you guys today before just jumping into some questions is, is this user determined email marketing thing? And this really again comes down to the psychology, right? So you can couple all these things together. You can couple the segmentation forms with the phishing, with the user determined email marketing. And that's when you get amazing, amazing results. When I'm running a phishing campaign with these three lists, when I'm running a solid campaign with this men's list that ROI really hard, it's a user determined email marketing campaign. And we're in the top 10 on the launch there. And then we're also running one on a supplement down here, we're in the top 10 on the launch over there. That's when people start emailing you going, how the hell are you on all these different lists at the same time? And I watched you email some other offer. That's not even one of these offers. You segment it out and you just do this kind of stuff, right? So anyways, it sounds kind of complicated, but I promise you that it's really not as hard as you think. It really comes down to segmentation, building that relationship with your list through your autoresponder series, learning how to communicate with them in such a way that they feel like you're really developing stronger for on a day to day basis. Fishing, and then once you find the big fish, dump these user determined email marketing campaigns on top of it and then hit it as hard as you possibly can. And you will get the serious revenue out of your list. So, user determined email market. Let's dig in here. Can you guys see this big goofy flow chart here? So this is just based on like a VSL promo. Let's say we were running a four day VSL promo. This is the type of campaign we will create for the people who basically raised their hand and said they're interested in the software. So a simple example is email one over here is always going to be a curiosity based email. And the reason why is I want to know who's curious about abs or flexibility or fat burning pills or whatever it is that we may be selling, right? I want to know who's curious about that. So we create a curiosity email that is supposed to push a few pain buttons and really peak their interest to click on this link and check out the product. We'll resend anybody who didn't open to unopens to get as many people segmented in these clicked or open categories as possible. That may be a one day process where we send the email and we resend in the afternoon and opens. It may be a week or two week long process where we send the email three days later, we resend unopens, three days later, we resend unopens. So we get the maximum ROI of one email possible and we get the maximum amount of people under these clicked and open buckets possible because that's where the magic really starts to happen. So I'll give you an example of this, right? Let's say email one is like, Hey, you know, I just discovered this new free video that tells you a 30 seconds body weight trick that you could do sitting down in a chair that will shrink your waistline by two inches and give you an awesome butt, right? If you'd be interested in checking out this new video, click the link here and you can check out this new video. That's a terrible email. I would never write like that, but let's just use it as an example, right? You resend unopens a couple of times and then you discover that the people have clicked, you segment them up and the people have opened and you segment them up. Now, let's just take it from forget this, forget this whole side over here. Just take it from the clicked and open side. If somebody opened the email and they clicked on it, that tells us they're interested in the topic that you just showed them, right? And if somebody opened the email and didn't click, that tells us that they're either reluctant or not interested in the topic that you just showed them. So the psychology of these emails is significantly different. This person already checked out the offer, right? So if they checked out the offer, what's the next email you should be sending them? Is it another curiosity email? No, because they are already seeing the offer. You don't need them to see the offer. You need them to buy the offer. So email 2A here is what we call overcoming objections, just like face to face sales, sales letters, anything like that, right? I want to overcome objections. Now, since I created this one, this template for VSLs in particular, this is what we run for our VSL promos when they do well. This email 2A is overcoming objections of a VSL. The objection for a VSL is always the video, right? This is a powerful tip that everybody who's listening will make a lot of money on. So here's how this email goes. Hey, Kevin, I saw you click the link to check out the flatten your waist by two inches and build an awesome butt video. And I kind of hate watching these videos too. They're annoying and they're too long. So I went ahead and called my friend over at the whatever website.com and he sent me a link to the text page where you can read it at your own convenience. Here's the link for you. And then you write HTTP.com. You make it look like a real website. You hyperlink it with your affiliate link or whatever link you might be doing. Check it out at your own convenience. Thanks. You're sign off one, right? So this is amazing, right? You get such a huge ROI on an email where you're basically, they're thanking you. Thank you, Tyler, for sending me the text version of this sales page, right? Now, here's the crazy data here, guys. This is worth 30% of our total sales of the promo. This one email. Because ultimately, what I've determined is, you know, in my own mind is 30% of the people will just never watch the damn video. And you get them a text page and they're like, Hey, I'll buy it off a text page. So giving them an option is huge. And sometimes people don't have text pages for BSL. So I'm like, whatever is your loss? It's 30% of a sales bump for free as long as somebody understands this concept, right? So this email is really powerful. So let's go down to opens now. The psychology of people who opened but didn't click. So if we want to really see, we want to know, they opened, they were curious because the subject line, they didn't click. Maybe they're interested, maybe they're not. So we want to give them another chance, right? And so email to be, it's not going to be overcoming objections. It's going to be telling them that everybody else clicked and why didn't they, right? So email to be is what I call my social pressure email, which is a very short email that says, you know, Hey, Mark, you know, the other day I sent out this email about this great video where you could, you know, shrink two inches off your waistline and build an awesome butt and 32,163 people already checked it out. Did you? Question mark? If not, you better check it out now. Here's the link, right? Because they're like, Oh, wow, everybody else checked it out. Well, I have nine. And then they click and those emails, these two emails right here, so powerful. It's so frightening because they're just simple, they're personal and they get people to do the action you want. This comes back to strategy. What is the purpose of the open email but not click is to get them to click. What's the purpose of this email right here is to get them to remove objections. And if this is in the VSL promo, then you have to look at the sales copy and find out what the objections are. What are the objections? Is it the cost? Is it is it the product size? What is it? And then overcome that objection with this email and essentially all this other stuff right here that you see is just a repetition of that same process. So the people who opened this email, they get sent forward to the next email. The people who click this email, they get sent forward to the next email. The unopened, they get re sent this email until they open this email. If they're on email to be once they check out the offer, then they get forward enough to email that tells them the overcoming objections. And same thing here, email 3D. We tend to send them content because I like a mix of, I like a nice little roller coaster mix of content. Get the click. We'll send them a little bit more content, re send that unopens and then if they open or click them, we'll send them up to the more content piece because we know they're content people because they responded to content. So this is kind of the way I like to run my email marketing campaigns once we determine what an offer is already doing well. So again, phishing first, find the offer that does well, then spend the time to build these big campaigns. Because I've done the opposite. Like I said, I know what not to do. I've built the big campaign with the offer that I hadn't tested yet and then the ROI was terrible and I wasted all this time on building something and then the next day I sent an email, a phishing offer and one of the five emails did better ROI than the entire launch campaign, right? So it's just a matter of really determining the offer that your people resonate with and then spending the time to build the stuff out. Because people ask me, how often do you do this stuff? Well, I do it when it's important, when it's time to make revenue. But when we're phishing, we're just phishing. We're sending emails, we're not doing a user-determined email marketing. We're just trying to find out what our people want, right? And once we do, we direct all the traffic to it, even some of the traffic that might not be interested to give them a chance to raise their hand, right? Give them a chance to raise their hand. If they don't raise their hand and email one from that chart, then we just pick them off the list and we say, time to put them into the next phishing bucket, right? This way you can mail so many different things at the same time. You can learn so much more data and knowledge about your list. But I mean, if you guys take anything away from this, just run that click email to a VSO and you just be like, this is worth the full price of this thing. My bar is crazy. This is crazy. People love you for it. I swear, every time I send an email, like, thanks so much, Tyler. I really don't like watching videos. It's great. I know. So, yeah, that's basically what I do in a nutshell, guys. And like I said, I think you have to change it for different strategies based on how often you're mailing, based on the type of promos you're doing, are you doing launches, are you doing sequential campaigns, time cap things, product volume cap things. So, there is a lot of wiggle room here. But like I said, I've consulted with people who mail aggressive third party offers and I've consulted with people who mail content once a week and then open up a big giant launch shopping cart twice a year for like a week, right? So, this applies to any business. Ultimately, you're learning how to communicate with your customers in a way that they feel heard. And that's what people really want. They want to feel heard by you. They don't want to be a part of this giant cow pasture. They want to be your star, you know, your star performer. So, that's what this does. If I email you the next day after you clicked and you didn't click, right? And you get an email that's, hey, I saw you click the link and here's this text page. You're like, oh, thanks. And the other guy gets a click and he's like, oh, I haven't checked it out yet. You know, we're presupposing they haven't checked it out yet. Oh, no, I haven't checked it out yet. I got to click this link, right? And then the next day, they click the link and they get the email that says, oh, I say, I said, you click the link, right? They're feeling really emotionally attached to you because you're taking them through the ride that you want them to go on. Not this sequential ride where they just might pop up in the middle of some campaign that won't make sense to them. Brilliant. Tyler, I got a couple of questions. I'm sure Kevin does too. The very first email that they receive, the welcome email, do you have, we spoke about, who was Andre? He has this trick where he gets them to already whitelist them for the very first email. He teaches them what to do. Right. Something like that. Yeah. So the whitelisting comes down to adding the name, the contacts to the email address and adding the folder, getting people to file away on the folder and getting to respond. Those are kind of my big three things there. And of course, if you're on an email service provider, we can get them double opt in, getting them double opt in. You know, InfusionStop has a feature where you can get them to click a link to access something and then it'll double opt them in and send them to a page where it's like a sandwich page between accessing something. So it's a pretty fun way to do it in InfusionStop. You kind of got to just know your broadcast software in order to figure out the tricks to doing that. But back to the autoresponder piece. Yeah, it really comes down to add your email to their contacts list, give them a reason to open every day and file that email away into a folder and then respond to you. Those are kind of my big three pieces to get people to engage with you in such a way that the inboxes actually don't get too pissed about you sending emails every day to their email inbox. Now that double opt in is interesting. Kevin and I went back and forward with that for a little bit. Yeah. But you're talking about using technology to double opt in or the old traditional double opt in. They get it in the email box. Yeah, it's kind of a traditional double opt in. It's a little bit more of a sneaky double opt in, if you will. It's more like, hey, Kevin, I got this free 12 minute flexibility program for you. Click the link right here and it's yours. And they click the link, it double ops them in, puts them on a sandwich page and says, thanks for confirming your email. Here's the 12 stretches page or whatever. And you click that and you fix it. So you can run that within Infusionsoft, not through broadcast or not through autoresponder campaigns, but through broadcast sequences. So you can time it after launches or whatever to drive out right after an autoresponder ends with like 10,000 new customers and you can opt them in and then resend that and opens over and over again until you get the maximum volume of people double opt in and do this while they feel good about it. And do you prefer Infusionsoft or is that they call it? I like Infusionsoft. I like Infusionsoft. I really do. It has a lot of really amazing features, especially for the user to determine email sequences. And I think it's great to a point. We're sending, we're like about sending 5 million emails a month right now. And Infusionsoft is not going to be able to handle our volume very much longer. So I think we're going to have to jump ship and go with the bigger company like Merrill Post or something along those lines, which I've heard just wonderful things about. So yeah, totally. And do you have a lot of people swear by sending their first email out before 8.30 a.m.? Do you get any thoughts on the first email and open rates? Yeah, so we've tested a lot of different open rates across the board. I think the big thing is getting people used to a time that's comfortable for you. What I do is I look at my industry. So if you're mailing third party offers, then there's a good chance that you have kind of like a lot of people on a lot of people's lists. And so what I try to do is look at my industry. And for a long time when I first got in this game, I noticed that everybody sent it midnight. Right? Midnight. Everybody sent it midnight. Everybody sent it midnight. I was like, well, I'll send it 12.30. And you guys will get 30 minutes of people and then I'll get every single person for the next 24 hours because the first you don't open your phone and scroll to the bottom. You open your phone and you look at the first email in your phone. Right? So they had all their emails down here and mine was on top. Right? And so then I started seeing, oh, now they're mailing at 1.15. So I got to change that. They let it 1.30 now. Right? So it's really a matter of like, I look at my industry in particular to see what the main, their competitors, their friends and competitors at the same time, I guess you will. But I try to look at when they're mailing and I try to appeal as close to after them as possible to maximize the amount of time that I get the traffic and minimize the amount of time that they get the traffic. So it's not a pretty answer in terms of like, what the maximum or what the maximum open rate time is, but it is as far as the ROI you get on the list because the people who are opening aren't reading somebody else's email about the same topic first. Right? Sure. And the launch sequence that you spoke about, how did you overcome people who enter the launch sequence halfway through who didn't get the full story? So you're talking about when I run these user determined pieces right here, these big, these big pieces. So they don't, they never, they never see it. Right? So if you, if you, if you get the curiosity email and you get that email three times and you don't ever open it, you don't ever click on it, you don't care about what I'm talking about. So we take all the people who clicked and open and we dump them through the rest and then we take the people who didn't open and we either dump them into some sort of like build a relationship because obviously they're not opening and clicking campaign or we just start fishing with those, those people as well. So I don't, I've got just here some notes and then they finish that the, you're fishing analogy. We do exactly the same and it's amazing. I mean, Christine's part of that team, but we'll have three people write three different versions of something to go fish like your analogy there. Yeah. And the, um, the, uh, once they're on the buyer's list, Tyler, you start all over again with a whole different, uh, sequence and personalization. Yeah. So just take them a little bit deeper. Usually the leads lists are geared towards customer acquisition. So they're usually like cookie timers and, you know, like buy or a little bit more buy or die kind of stuff. Like, Hey, you just joined the list. Here's some content. Here's a great piece. Here's a curiosity email. Hey, this sale is disappearing. Buy or die kind of thing. Cookie timer. And then if they buy, then we upscale them to the customer list and it's more of like an introduction, build relationship piece, but, you know, it comes back to a strategy. The majority of what we focus on is customer acquisition, not lead acquisition. We do have a few lead campaigns, but the majority of what we do is customer acquisition. If the majority was lead campaigns, we'd spend a lot more time on trying to figure out exactly how to get these leads and the customers. But for the time being, what we do is we, we run them through some sort of campaign to try to get them into customers. And then we run a mix of third party offers and internal offers, typically very aggressive internal offers to the leads list because we want them to become customers and escalate themselves to the next level. It says basically the question of the book is what one thing, if you accomplished it would make everything else seem totally worthless. Right. And I love that concept because to me at the time I was trying to run a YouTube and a Facebook, I was getting so scattered brain. And this is when I really dug into this concept of strategy. Like what was my strategy at that point? My strategy was like do everything and see what works. And like, you don't have enough time to do all that. So, so it really was like, Hey, what one thing could I really figure out? Okay, let's figure out an awesome funnel that we can start driving media to. Let's figure out the perfect product to launch to our affiliate traffic. Right. And it's just like you find that one thing and you just do it and you do it and you do it until you figure it out. And then once you do, then it's time to allow yourself to move on to the next thing. But I think so many of us, especially entrepreneurs, we get distracted really easily by the next shiny object. And by taking that one thing and writing it up on your walls and posting it on cards and just being like, dude, win or lose, I'm going all in on this one thing until I get it solved. You know, what's your unique sales pitch? What's your competitive advantage? I had a copywriter recently really impressed me by emailing me and saying, hey, can I write, you know, a second sales letter for this particular offer you have? And you'll run a test on it to your traffic. And if I beat you, I get to keep anything over the control. And I said, oh, shit, that's a pretty good deal. I'll run that. I got nothing to lose in everything to gain. I don't really care about front-end revenue. I care about customer acquisition. So I'm just like, hey, if you want to play that game, I'll play that game with you. And there's so much better than somebody emailing you. Are you in need of a copywriter? I charge $1,000 for an article. Well, you know, I mean, I tried to make that deal with some guys over at Agora. I said the exact same thing. If I beat your control, actually, it was more generous. I said, can I have half the difference? And they're like, no, it was like, really? Kevin, any time you want to make that deal, you come to me. All right. OK. You asked me, I think strategy. I think strategy, Mark, that's the big thing. Strategy. But what's the what's the end game there? You're trying to be a copywriter. You're trying to do what are you trying to do? Find the people who are doing exactly what you want to do. Bisec their strategy and repeat their strategy. I mean, that's the easiest way to do it. You know, when I started grinding my strategy down from just try everything in the world and like scatterbrained business, I was still making multiple six figures, but it was just to scatterbrained down to like, here's what we do and this is what we're going to do. And then when we're going to do something else, we're just going to copy this model to here and scale that vertical at the same time. And when I when I really started studying the people who were doing the best, it was like, God, their business model is so much easier. You know, like I have a real good media buyer friend and we just started buying a lot more media and I'm like, all worried about like, I'm like, what are these tracking links? How is this one's not carrying over to here? And this one over there, he's like, do, do, do, do. I'm done. How much money did you spend at the end of the month? How much money did you make at the end of the month? That's the only metric I care about. He's like, am I making my money back? I'm winning. That's all I care about. You know, so it was good. It was good to calm me down because I don't need to be the media buyer, the guy who watches over the media buyer, the sales funnel builder, the data. I don't need to do all that stuff. I need to have people that I trust doing that stuff. And so sometimes we get so heady about controlling everything. And the most beneficial thing that's happened to me in the last time, you know, six months or a year and hiring more people was letting go. You know, there's a guy out there who's going to make a better VS album. You know what I mean? There's a guy out there who's going to analyze data and write code and build web pages way the hell better than I could ever do. You know, so get that strategy down and then figure out who's doing exactly what you want to do and copy their stuff. And work your butt off. That's pretty good. I had to work my butt off for two years before I could start hiring people and like chilling. And now I can now I can relax a little bit more. So good. And you deserve it.
